danger_signals.visual_form: Fighting stage environment where characters battle
danger_signals.meaning: Active danger state - characters are in direct physical conflict

danger_signals.visual_form: Character roster represents competing family members and outsiders
danger_signals.meaning: Political danger - power vacuum and internal family strife over leadership

safety_signals.visual_form: Oume Gōketsuji as unplayable boss character observing tournament
safety_signals.meaning: Safe authority figure - current clan leader watching succession trials

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Palette-swap between Oume and Otane (sister relationship creates visual distinction)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tournament setting as clan internal affair - Japanese family dojo/arena implied

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Diverse international character roster (Italian, English, implied others)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinct character designs separate outsiders from clan members
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: International participants in clan tournament suggests open selection process

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tournament bracket system with eight selectable characters competing; Oume appears as final unplayable boss encounter
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Characters fight through tournament progression to prove worthiness for clan leadership; each character has specific motivations and win quotes reflecting their stake in succession

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Otane (younger sister) depicted as playable tournament combatant competing against her abuser/sister Oume
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Otane's character arc shows transformation from abuse victim to trained fighter; her presence in tournament indicates family struggle for dominance

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Roster includes non-Japanese characters (Angela Belti from Italy, Annie Hamilton from England, Keith Wayne) mixed with clan members
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: External characters have personal motivations for entering tournament (Keith seeks to prove himself to Annie; others seek personal advancement)

environmental_storytelling.example: Pre-fight screens in Japanese versions display character biographies, contextualizing each fighter's motivation and social standing within tournament narrative

Each character originally had specific win quotes for each defeated opponent (Japanese version), creating narrative depth about inter-character relationships and clan politics; North American localization reduced to single generic win quotes per character, diminishing storytelling
Oume and Otane share visual design with palette-swap distinction, immediately communicating their familial relationship and similar power level

Japanese version includes scene where Oume speaks to player before fight, establishing her as current authority; this scene changes based on opponent (sister Otane vs. others), further emphasizing family dynamics

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Appears only as unplayable boss character; positions herself as judge/obstacle to succession; dialogue changes contextually when facing sister Otane
Transformed from neglected child into tournament combatant; actively competes to defeat her abuser and prove leadership worthiness; her participation signals internal clan power struggle
Participates in tournament driven by personal achievement rather than clan connection; her Italian strongwoman identity marks her as outsider with independent motivations
Enters tournament specifically to prove worthiness to Annie Hamilton (fellow competitor); his behavior reflects personal romantic stakes rather than clan succession
Rich, spoiled English competitor who loves animals; her participation suggests personal reasons; her presence attracts Keith's attention, establishing inter-character dynamics

notes: Power Instinct communicates world-state primarily through narrative framing (succession tournament) rather than dynamic visual environment shifts. Danger is signaled through combat itself and character relationships. Faction identity relies on character design distinctiveness and family relationships (palette-swaps for blood relatives). The game's absurdist humor tone suggests that serious political/familial conflict is undercut by comedic characterization. Regional localization differences (Japanese vs. North American versions) significantly impact environmental storytelling depth - Japanese version provides richer narrative context through character biographies and differentiated win quotes. Tournament structure itself serves as primary world-state communicator. Transformation feature mentioned in series overview suggests characters shift visual appearance and fighting style through certain moves, but specific manifestation details not provided in available source.
